steps.title: Look at what sits on each side of the blank
steps.content: Before the blank: "Butterfly... attempts to make use of the entire visual light spectrum" — a complete independent clause that makes a general claim. After the blank: "sporting rainbow-striped goggles, the rainbow-hued swimmer splashes through a wavy rainbow of water" — another complete independent clause that specifies HOW the painting uses the spectrum.

steps.title: Pick the right punctuation between them
steps.content: A colon joins two independent clauses when the second one elaborates on or explains the first. That is exactly what is happening here: the swimmer-and-water description spells out the spectrum claim.

steps.title: Eliminate the other choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘spectrum’ (no punctuation): fuses two independent clauses with nothing between them — a run-on. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘spectrum:’: a colon correctly introduces the elaboration that follows. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘spectrum while’: "while" without a preceding comma fails to mark the clause boundary, and it forces a temporal/contrast reading rather than the elaboration the sentence is actually making. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘spectrum, while’: a comma + subordinator is grammatical, but "while" signals simultaneity/contrast, not the "here's how" elaboration the sentence requires. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
